title: Missing flood data hits 25,000
full_text:
More than 25,000 Logan property owners, caught in a flood-mapping crisis, have been told flawed council maps over-estimated water levels by 1m.
The extraordinary finding emerged during a special Logan City Council meeting on Wednesday after councillors formally received an independent review into the controversial flood modelling under-pinning the draft Logan Plan.
The review, prepared by interstate consultancy Restore Blue Advisory Services and finalised on April 24, found some flood levels associated with a 1 per cent annual chance of flooding in populated downstream urban areas “may have been over-estimated by approximately 1m or more”.
Councillors also backed Mayor Jon Raven writing to Deputy Premier Jarrod Bleijie seeking support to remove the controversial probable maximum flood mapping from council planning instruments.
The controversy erupted in 2020 after updated flood overlays linked to the draft Logan Plan identified additional properties as flood-affected, sparking fears over insurance costs, property values and future development restrictions.
A council officer report tabled for Wednesday’s special meeting, titled Logan and Albert Rivers flood model independent review findings, explained the Logan and Albert Rivers flood study was originally completed by WRM Water and Environment in 2014 before a new study began in 2019 using updated national flood guidelines.
Logan Ratepayers Association president Brett Raguse said residents remained deeply concerned about the fallout from the flood overlay saga despite Wednesday’s meeting.
“While we are disappointed the independent review does not go far enough to address the financial and emotional impacts facing residents, today’s meeting was an important first step by council in formally receiving the review,” Mr Raguse said. “We now look forward to the special council meeting on June 4 and further discussion around council’s proposed way forward.”
